audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for parable discourse: sower, hearing/understanding, Isaiah citation, seed/soil, weeds, mustard, leaven, hidden treasure, pearl, net, householder, and Nazareth rejection.

verses.restored: On that day Jesus went out of the house and sat beside the sea.
Great crowds gathered to him, so that he entered a boat and sat, and all the crowd stood on the shore.
He spoke many things to them in parables, saying, Look, the sower went out to sow.
As he sowed, some fell beside the way, and the birds came and devoured them.
Other seed fell on rocky places where it did not have much soil, and immediately it sprang up because it had no depth of soil.
When the sun rose, it was scorched, and because it had no root, it withered.
Other seed fell among thorns, and the thorns came up and choked them.
Other seed fell on beautiful/good soil and gave fruit: some hundredfold, some sixty, some thirty.
The one having ears, let him hear.
The disciples came and said to him, Why do you speak to them in parables?
He answered, To you it has been given to know the mysteries of the reign of the heavens, but to them it has not been given.
For whoever has, more will be given and he will overflow; but whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken from him.
Because of this I speak to them in parables: because seeing they do not see, and hearing they do not hear or understand.
In them the prophecy of Isaiah is fulfilled: With hearing you will hear and not understand, and seeing you will see and not perceive.

For this people's heart has grown thick; with their ears they hardly hear, and their eyes they have closed, lest they see with the eyes, hear with the ears, understand with the heart, turn back, and I heal them.
But blessed/flourishing are your eyes because they see, and your ears because they hear.
Amen, I say to you: many prophets and righteous ones desired to see what you see and did not see it, and to hear what you hear and did not hear it.
You, then, hear the parable of the sower.
When anyone hears the word of the reign and does not understand, the harmful one comes and seizes what was sown in his heart; this is what was sown beside the way.
The one sown on rocky places is the one hearing the word and immediately receiving it with joy,
yet he has no root in himself but is temporary; when pressure or persecution happens because of the word, immediately he stumbles.
The one sown among thorns is the one hearing the word, but the worry of the age and the deceit of wealth choke the word, and it becomes fruitless.
The one sown on beautiful/good soil is the one hearing the word and understanding it, who indeed bears fruit and produces: one hundredfold, one sixty, one thirty.
He set another parable before them, saying, The reign of the heavens is like a person sowing beautiful/good seed in his field.
While people slept, his enemy came, sowed weeds among the wheat, and went away.
When the grass sprouted and made fruit, then the weeds appeared also.
The servants of the householder came and said, Lord, did you not sow beautiful/good seed in your field? From where, then, does it have weeds?
He said to them, An enemy person did this. The servants said, Then do you want us to go and gather them?
He said, No, lest while gathering the weeds you uproot the wheat with them.
Let both grow together until the harvest; at harvest time I will say to the harvesters, Gather first the weeds and bind them into bundles to burn, but gather the wheat into my barn.
He set another parable before them, saying, The reign of the heavens is like a mustard seed, which a person took and sowed in his field.
It is smaller than all seeds, but when grown it is greater than the garden plants and becomes a tree, so that the birds of the heavens come and nest in its branches.
He spoke another parable to them: The reign of the heavens is like leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of flour until it was all leavened.
All these things Jesus spoke to the crowds in parables, and without a parable he spoke nothing to them,
so that what was spoken through the prophet might be fulfilled: I will open my mouth in parables; I will utter things hidden from the foundation of the world.
Then he left the crowds and went into the house. His disciples came to him, saying, Explain to us the parable of the weeds of the field.
He answered, The one sowing the beautiful/good seed is the Son of Humanity.
The field is the world; the beautiful/good seed are the sons of the reign; the weeds are the sons of the harmful one.
The enemy who sowed them is the adversarial one; the harvest is the completion of the age, and the harvesters are messengers.
Just as weeds are gathered and burned with fire, so it will be at the completion of the age.
The Son of Humanity will send his messengers, and they will gather out of his reign all stumbling-blocks and those doing lawlessness.
They will cast them into the furnace of fire; there will be weeping and grinding of teeth.
Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the reign of their Father. The one having ears, let him hear.
The reign of the heavens is like treasure hidden in the field, which a person found and hid; in his joy he goes and sells all he has and buys that field.
Again, the reign of the heavens is like a merchant seeking beautiful pearls.
Finding one very precious pearl, he went away and sold all he had and bought it.
Again, the reign of the heavens is like a net cast into the sea and gathering from every kind.
When it was filled, they drew it onto the shore, sat down, gathered the good into containers, and threw out the rotten.
So it will be at the completion of the age: the messengers will go out and separate the harmful from among the righteous.
They will cast them into the furnace of fire; there will be weeping and grinding of teeth.
Have you understood all these things? They said to him, Yes.
He said to them, Therefore every scribe discipled to the reign of the heavens is like a householder who brings out of his treasure things new and old.
When Jesus finished these parables, he departed from there.
Coming into his homeland, he taught them in their synagogue, so that they were astonished and said, From where does this one have this wisdom and these powers?
Is this not the craftsman's son? Is not his mother called Mary, and his brothers James, Joseph, Simon, and Judas?
And are not all his sisters with us? From where, then, does this one have all these things?
They stumbled over him. But Jesus said to them, A prophet is not dishonored except in his homeland and in his house.
He did not do many powers there because of their lack of trust.
